channel.title: FT – Fiction
channel.description: Fiction reviews and articles from the Financial Times.

item.title: The best books of the week
item.description: From Vance vs Pence to Big Tech, Garry Sobers and King Juan Carlos. Plus novels by Avni Doshi and Daniel Mason, ’80s and ’90s music journalism — and Suzi Feay’s pick of debut fiction

item.title: The Mulai — an extraplanetary experiment in language and identity
item.description: Munir Hachemi’s playful, hedonistic novel on descendants of colonisers from Earth offers a daring exercise in translation

item.title: The First House by Avni Doshi — darkness in the domestic
item.description: When her marriage collapses, a woman seeks meaning in astrology, mythology and obsession in a fever-dream novel by the author of Booker-shortlisted ‘Burnt Sugar’

item.title: Country People by Daniel Mason — freewheeling fun in the backwoods of Vermont
item.description: An academic family’s pursuit of the bucolic life takes a strange and whimsical detour into folklore in a novel that makes for perfect summer escapism

item.title: One Hundred Guinea Pigs by Gustavo Rodríguez — a surprisingly life-affirming tale of assisted dying
item.description: A bittersweet novel about ageing and end-of-life care set in conservative Peru, a country full of contradictions

item.title: Five of the best debut novels — lust, longing and a wild night out on the Curry Mile
item.description: First-time tales of romantic obsession and the problem with polyamory — plus sibling relationships, secrets and swagger in Manchester and a winning story of dog-racing

item.title: The Anniversary — the messy reality of family and how to escape it
item.description: Andrea Bajani’s award-winning novel, newly translated from Italian, is a fearless, intimate tale of unhappy parents — and the release of leaving them behind

item.title: A tour of the world through bite-sized fiction
item.description: A new Penguin anthology celebrates the universal appeal of the short story with 34 fine examples from dozens of countries

item.title: Interview.Swiss novelist Christian Kracht: ‘I decided not to be a cynic any more’
item.description: With his breakthrough hit ‘Eurotrash’ set to appear on stage and screen, and new novel ‘Air’ out this month, the one-time enfant terrible is conquering the English-speaking world

item.title: A cold war-set debut, plus a superhero satire turns savage — the best new science-fiction books
item.description: A British spy on a deep-sea vessel confronts supernatural forces while a heroine assumes an unexpected role in taking on ghostly villains

item.title: The End of Everything by M John Harrison — a dark vision of Britain’s decline
item.description: A characteristically apocalyptic novel is both a horrifying and compassionate view of an isolated nation where yuppie super-aliens live among humans

item.title: Exhibition — the uneasy boundary between art, identity and class
item.description: Alex Hyde’s novel about the relationship between a photographer and an artist with parallels to Tracey Emin raises questions about who gets to be seen as a genius

item.title: Long Wave — lost mothers and missing children
item.description: Daisy Johnson’s engaging yet elusive novel sets longing and trauma against a landscape of rivers, islands and unreliable memory

item.title: FT readers’ favourite books of 2026 so far
item.description: We asked you to share with us your favourite reads of the year so far — and here is what you told us

item.title: The best books for children and young adults to read this summer
item.description: James Lovegrove and Suzi Feay select their best mid-year reads

item.title: The best fiction and fiction in translation to read this summer
item.description: Maria Crawford and Ángel Gurría-Quintana select their best mid-year reads

item.title: The best science fiction and graphic novels to read this summer
item.description: James Lovegrove and Lucy Scholes select their best mid-year reads

item.title: The best literary non-fiction and poetry to read this summer
item.description: Carl Wilkinson and Maria Crawford select their best mid-year reads

item.title: The best audiobooks to listen to this summer
item.description: Alex Clark selects her best mid-year titles to listen to

item.title: The best crime and thriller books to read so far this year
item.description: Barry Forshaw and Adam LeBor select their best mid-year reads

item.title: Contrapposto by Dave Eggers — in defence of art without ambition
item.description: Eggers explores the rewards of the creative life and the compromises of artistic success in this unashamedly old-fashioned novel

item.title: The Vivisectors — campus novel meets omen of environmental doom
item.description: An untameable garden lays siege to an unnamed university town in Missouri Williams’s disquieting apocalyptic fable

item.title: Sail Away Land — weird tales of work, loss and loneliness
item.description: The strangeness of Ben Pester’s writing is not just decorative — it exposes how bizarre ordinary life already is

item.title: Atonement — ambitious adaptation can’t match Ian McEwan’s masterpiece
item.description: The Chichester Festival Theatre production has inventive touches but lacks intensity

item.title: The Typing Lady — short stories distil the essence of Ruth Ozeki’s fiction
item.description: An ambitious and playful first collection from the novelist ranges from the auto-fictional to the surreal
